LC90-1._model: 1990 Lamborghini Lola LC90 F1 | 1:43 | Blue | Onyx

DiabloP132-1._model: 1991 Lamborghini Diablo P132 | 1:43 | Blue | Leo Models
DiabloP132-1.short: Another model that was fully based off the Lamborghini Museum P132 car. There were many different development versions for the Diablo P132, and this is the last one! I rather like it.
DiabloP132-1.long: The Lamborghini Diablo P132 Prototype was the internal development designation for the very first Diablo project, created in the late 1980s as the successor to the Countach. It refers to the early Gandini-designed prototype phase before Chrysler restyled the car into the production Diablo we know today. These P132 cars were used for testing aerodynamics, cooling, and chassis behavior, and several different prototypes existed, including early rolling mules with partially finished bodywork and experimental engine cover concepts. Some early versions experimented with glass engine covers and extreme cooling layouts, but these ideas were later abandoned due to overheating issues. The surviving prototypes remain at Lamborghini's factory collection and were crucial in shaping the final 1990 Diablo design.

DiabloRoad-1._model: 1992 Lamborghini Diablo Roadster Prototype | 1:43 | Yellow | Leo Models
DiabloRoad-1.short: An accurate model of the Diablo Roadster Prototype, this model is based on the car that was sitting in the Lamborghini Museum before 2016.
DiabloRoad-1.long: The 1992 Lamborghini Diablo Roadster Prototype was the first official open-top study of the Diablo, unveiled at the Geneva Motor Show in 1992. It was designed to explore how a mid-engined V12 supercar could be adapted into a roadster without losing structural rigidity, which required significant chassis reinforcement. The prototype featured a redesigned rear engine cover with a central tunnel section to allow proper airflow and roof storage integration, along with larger side air intakes and revised rear deck ventilation. It also introduced interior and mirror design changes that would later influence production Diablos. Although it was never initially intended for production, it directly led to the Diablo VT Roadster that arrived in 1995.

RelDodVip-1._model: 1992 Lamborghini Dodge Viper RT/10 | 1:64 | Red | Hot Wheels

DiabloSE30-1.short: Not an impressive model, more of a toy, currently not 'fully' SE30, because the engine cover isn't accurate. That will be changed in the future, otherwise it's clearly an SE30. It's cool that the doors and headlights open. Let's also not forget that it's a vintage 90s model most likely.
DiabloSE30-1.long: The Lamborghini Diablo SE30 was introduced in 1993 to celebrate Lamborghini's 30th anniversary and became one of the most hardcore road-going Diablos of the era. The car was lighter, more powerful, and far more aggressive than the standard VT, using extensive weight reduction measures including thinner glass, manual windows on some cars, carbon fiber components, and a stripped-down interior. Its 5.7-liter V12 produced around 525 hp, and visually the SE30 introduced unique rear engine-cover intakes, special wheels, and distinctive purple launch colors that became closely associated with the model.

DiabloVT-1._model: 1993 Lamborghini Diablo VT | 1:43 | Metallic Red | AUTOart
DiabloVT-1.short: One of the best models in my collection for sure, it's an AUTOart diecast Diablo VT in this copper metallic color. After my resin models this is hands down the best 1:43 I have, it's just so detailed. Even the bottom is detailed and the front wheels (mostly) rotate! I love it, but it had arrived broken initially, that's why I had to reglue the side mirrors, twice!
DiabloVT-1.long: The Lamborghini Diablo VT was introduced in 1993 and became the first production Lamborghini with all-wheel drive. "VT" stood for "Viscous Traction," referring to the viscous center differential capable of sending torque to the front wheels when needed. Alongside the new drivetrain system, the VT introduced power steering for the first time on a Diablo, making the car far easier to drive than the original rear-wheel-drive 1990 version. The VT established the all-wheel-drive layout that would later define most flagship Lamborghini V12 models.

DiabloSE30J-1._model: 1994 Lamborghini Diablo SE30 Jota | 1:43 | Purple | Ixo
DiabloSE30J-1.short: An okay model of the insane SE30 Jota, it looks great on my shelf next to my other Diablos. But if we're being honest, Ixo didn't do the best job on this one. For starters, this is clearly supposed to be the Viola SE30 paint, but it's much darker, and the front proportions aren't the greatest...
DiabloSE30J-1.long: The Lamborghini Diablo SE30 Jota was an unofficial but factory-supported upgrade package created for SE30 owners seeking a more extreme racing-style configuration. The Jota conversion added open racing-style exhausts, revised engine management, larger roof-mounted intake scoops, freer-flowing induction systems, and significant performance upgrades that pushed output to around 595 hp. Unlike the normal SE30, the Jota was intended primarily for track use and sacrificed much of the remaining comfort equipment. The SE30 Jota became one of the wildest and most desirable Diablos ever built, directly inspiring later GT and GTR models.

DiabloSV-1.short: I honestly really like this Diablo SV toy by Welly in 1:38 scale. The model is actually really well detailed and I like the proportions. The doors open, and the rear is actually very detailed. Just look at that massive SV logo! A bargain for $8 shipped.
DiabloSV-1.long: The Lamborghini Diablo SV ("Super Veloce") debuted in 1995 as a more aggressive, rear-wheel-drive alternative to the all-wheel-drive VT. It used an upgraded version of the 5.7-liter V12 producing around 510 hp and focused on lighter weight, sharper handling, and a more raw driving experience. Visually, the SV introduced larger intakes, revised bumpers, an adjustable rear wing, and optional oversized "SV" side decals that became iconic. The SV also previewed styling cues later seen on the GT and GTR models.

DiabloValv-1._model: 1996 Lamborghini Diablo SE Jota Valvoline | 1:43 | White | Hachette
DiabloValv-1.short: Incredible model of the crazy Diablo SE Jota, also known as Diablo P.01 GT Jota, presented in the Valvoline livery here. Would've wished it was Rain-X, so it would match my Countach Rain-X, but oh well, it still looks cool, especially side by side!
DiabloValv-1.long: The Lamborghini Diablo SE Jota, often referred to as the "P.01," was a highly modified racing-oriented development based on the Diablo SE30 platform and created during Lamborghini's mid-1990s GT racing ambitions. The car became closely associated with Japanese racing and appeared in striking Rain-X and Valvoline liveries. Valvoline is the livery showed off here. Developed with endurance racing and GT1-style competition in mind, it featured extreme aerodynamic modifications, lightweight construction, enlarged cooling systems, racing suspension, and heavily upgraded V12 specifications. Although Lamborghini explored racing programs around the SE Jota concept, including Le Mans and GT-style projects, the programs remained limited and fragmented, making the P.01 one of the most obscure and mythical Diablo competition derivatives.

RelDodVip-2._model: 1996 Lamborghini Dodge Viper GTS | 1:64 | Yellow | Matchbox

RelDodVip-3._model: 1997 Lamborghini Dodge Viper GT2 | 1:64 | White | Maisto

RelDodVip-4._model: 1998 Lamborghini Dodge Viper GTS-R Concept | 1:64 | Red | Hot Wheels

DiabloGT-1._model: 1999 Lamborghini Diablo GT | 1:64 | Yellow | Stance Hunters
DiabloGT-1.short: Incredible model of the Diablo GT from Stance Hunters. The proportions, paint finish, interior look fantastic, and even the engine cover opens to reveal the magnificent V12. Limited Edition, 1 of 399
DiabloGT-1.long:
The Lamborghini Diablo GT was introduced in 1999 as the most extreme road-going Diablo ever built and represented Lamborghini's closest attempt at turning the Diablo into a street-legal race car. Developed from lessons learned through the Diablo GT2 and motorsport programs, the GT combined aggressive aerodynamics, major weight reduction, and a significantly upgraded V12 into a far more hardcore machine than the standard Diablo models. Only around 80 examples were officially produced for the European market.

Visually, the Diablo GT was dramatically different from a normal Diablo. It featured widened bodywork, a completely redesigned front bumper with large cooling ducts, NACA-style side intakes, a huge rear diffuser, center-mounted exhausts, and a large roof-mounted ram-air scoop feeding the engine. Much of the body used carbon fiber, and the car rode on special three-piece OZ wheels with an extremely aggressive stance.

The biggest upgrade was the engine. Lamborghini enlarged the V12 to 6.0 liters for the first time in a production Diablo, producing around 575 hp in European specification. Unlike the VT models, the GT remained rear-wheel drive to reduce weight and deliver a more raw driving experience. Inside, the cabin was stripped down with carbon fiber panels, racing bucket seats, harnesses, and reduced luxury equipment, although air conditioning remained standard.

The Diablo GT also directly led to the creation of the track-only Diablo GTR, which evolved the concept even further into a dedicated race car for Lamborghini's Supertrophy series. Today, the GT is widely considered one of the most iconic and desirable Diablos ever built, combining classic Marcello Gandini proportions with some of the wildest factory styling Lamborghini ever put on a road car.

DiabloGT2.short: I, for one, really like this orange Diablo GT2 Prototype car, it's just so radical. This here is the '2002' modification version, and the model is based on the exact modification that was sitting in the Lamborghini Museum!
DiabloGT2.long:
The Lamborghini Diablo GT2 was a factory-developed racing prototype created in 1997–1998 as Lamborghini's attempt to bring the Diablo into international GT competition, particularly the FIA GT Championship and potentially even Le Mans. Unlike the road-going Diablo GT that appeared later, the GT2 was a pure competition machine and one of the most extreme Diablos ever built.

The car was based on the Diablo SV/SV-R platform but heavily reworked with lightweight carbon fiber body panels, plexiglass side windows, a stripped racing interior, integrated roll cage, center-lock wheels, and aggressive aerodynamic modifications including a huge rear wing and redesigned front cooling ducts. It also became the first Lamborghini-developed Diablo to use a 6.0-liter V12, producing well over 600 horsepower in racing specification. This engine later directly influenced the production Diablo GT and Diablo VT 6.0. The prototype was first shown publicly at the 1997 Bologna Motor Show and later tested at circuits such as Varano and Monza in 1998. Lamborghini originally intended the GT2 to compete internationally, with some sources linking the project to possible Le Mans ambitions, but the program was ultimately canceled after Audi acquired Lamborghini in 1998. As a result, the GT2 never properly entered major FIA GT competition despite extensive development work.

Only a tiny number of Diablo GT2 cars were built, making it one of the rarest factory Lamborghini race projects ever created. Many of its design ideas and engineering solutions later evolved into the road-going Diablo GT and the track-only Diablo GTR.

It was kept in the Lamborghini Museum from 2002 until 2015.

DiabloVTRoad-1._model: 1999 Lamborghini Diablo VT Roadster | 1:43 | Orange | Edicola
DiabloVTRoad-1.short: I quite like this model, the proportions are mostly nice, but I'm not a big fan of the headlights. They don't feel realistic enough, but hey, it's just a $10-15 model, so can one really complain?
DiabloVTRoad-1.long:
The 1999 Lamborghini Diablo VT Roadster was the updated open-top version of the Diablo introduced after Lamborghini's Audi-era facelift of the model line. While the original VT Roadster had debuted in 1995, the 1999 version received major visual and technical updates that modernized the Diablo for the end of the 1990s.

The most noticeable change was the replacement of the classic pop-up headlights with fixed units sourced from the Nissan 300ZX, improving aerodynamics and bringing the design more in line with contemporary supercars. The car also received redesigned bumpers, larger 18-inch wheels, bigger brakes with ABS, a revised dashboard, integrated airbags, and updated interior ergonomics influenced by Audi's early involvement with Lamborghini.

Mechanically, it retained the 5.7-liter V12 with all-wheel drive ("VT" standing for Viscous Traction), but power increased to around 530 hp thanks to the addition of variable valve timing. Performance improved accordingly, with top speed rising to approximately 335 km/h (208 mph).

Like earlier VT Roadsters, it featured a removable carbon-fiber targa roof panel stored above the engine cover rather than a folding soft top. The large side intakes, exposed rear deck design, and aggressive proportions gave the VT Roadster a very different appearance from the coupe, making it one of the most recognizable Diablos ever produced.

DiabloVTRRoad-1._model: 1999 Lamborghini Diablo VT-R Roadster | 1:43 | White | Leo Models
DiabloVTRRoad-1.short: I really like this VT-R Roadster (often called the Diablo Roadster-R), it's basically an open top version of the Diablo SVR, but only one of these were ever built!
DiabloVTRRoad-1.long:
The Lamborghini Diablo VT-R Roadster was a unique one-off factory special built in 1997 as an open-top racing-oriented version of the Diablo, created mainly to promote Lamborghini's single-make racing activities and the Diablo SV-R program. It was based on the early Diablo VT Roadster platform but heavily modified into a much more extreme machine.

Unlike the standard all-wheel-drive VT Roadster, the VT-R Roadster surprisingly used rear-wheel drive only and featured a heavily tuned 5.7-liter V12 producing nearly 600 hp in full race specification. The car also received major visual changes including headlight-delete air intakes, racing bucket seats with harnesses, lightweight body modifications, slick tires, and competition-style aerodynamic components.

Only a single Diablo VT-R Roadster was ever built, making it one of the rarest factory Diablo specials. It effectively combined the open-top styling of the VT Roadster with the aggressive motorsport direction of the SV-R and GT racing Diablos developed during the late 1990s.

DiabloGTR-1.short: An unusual 1/72 scale Lamborghini Diablo GT-R. It was used when I bought it.
DiabloGTR-1.long:
The Lamborghini Diablo GTR was introduced in 1999 as the ultimate track-only evolution of the Diablo and the true successor to the Diablo SV-R race cars used in the Lamborghini Supertrophy series. Based on the road-going Diablo GT, the GTR was transformed into a full competition machine with a stripped interior, integrated roll cage, center-lock magnesium wheels, racing brakes, pneumatic air jacks, and a massive rear wing mounted directly to the chassis.

Its 6.0-liter V12 was heavily upgraded with individual throttle bodies, titanium connecting rods, revised engine management, variable valve timing, and a dynamic intake system, producing around 590 hp. Unlike most later Diablos, the GTR remained rear-wheel drive and was designed purely for circuit use, never being road legal. Around 40 examples were ultimately produced between 1999 and 2000, and the model competed successfully in the Lamborghini Supertrophy and Australian Nations Cup racing.

DiabloGTRS-1.short: The Diablo GTR-S was built by Reiter Engineering, and it just looks so incredible with that large Lamborghini Logo on the front food. An even more radical version of the GT-R.
DiabloGTRS-1.long: The Lamborghini Diablo GTR-S by Reiter Engineering refers to an unofficial but well-known evolution of Diablo GT-style race conversions carried out by Reiter Engineering, the German motorsport specialist closely tied to Lamborghini GT racing programs. Building on the Diablo GT and GTR race platforms of the late 1990s, Reiter developed further track-focused modifications for customer and racing use, refining aerodynamics, suspension setups, and weight reduction strategies for GT competition. These cars were not factory production variants but competition evolutions based on Diablo race chassis used in Supertrophy and GT racing contexts, often adapted for specific series requirements or private teams. In practice, the GTR-S designation is used to describe these upgraded Reiter-built race evolutions rather than a single official Lamborghini model.

Diablo6.0-1.short: One of my favourite Lamborghini models ever. The 6.0 Diablo just hits different. It's my favorite version of the Diablo and that's almost certantly because I used to always use it as a child in Need For Speed: Hot Pursuit 2, and always wanted a model of it. The model itself is nice, besides the front, sorry. The front proportions are off, the headlights are poor, but otherwise it's incredible for the price.
Diablo6.0-1.long: The Lamborghini Diablo VT 6.0 arrived in 2000 as the final major production evolution of the Diablo and the first version heavily revised under Audi ownership. While it retained the classic Diablo silhouette, the car received redesigned front and rear bumpers, updated air intakes, improved cooling, modernized interior quality, and major mechanical refinements. The 6.0-liter V12 produced around 543 hp, and Audi's involvement dramatically improved reliability, build quality, and drivability compared to earlier Diablos. Many enthusiasts consider the VT 6.0 the best-balanced and most usable Diablo ever built.

Diablo6.0SE-1._model: 2000 Lamborghini Diablo VT 6.0 SE | 1:64 | Gold | F-Toys
Diablo6.0SE-1.short: This model started off as an Orange Diablo VT 6.0 by F-Toys, but I decided to repaint it in gold, for it to represent the incredible Diablo 6.0 SE, which I have actually seen in the Lamborghini Museum during my first visit in 2016! It turned out very well, you can see the interior in more detail below, as well as it, before it was modified. I had only gotten it for just $8, but not in perfect condition.
Diablo6.0SE-1.long: The Lamborghini Diablo VT 6.0 SE was introduced in 2001 as the final special edition of the Diablo before production ended in favor of the Murciélago. Only 42 examples were built, split between two exclusive colors: Oro Elios (gold) representing sunrise and Marrone Eklipsis (brown metallic) representing sunset. Mechanically identical to the standard VT 6.0, the SE mainly focused on exclusivity, unique trim details, special badging, and upgraded interiors, marking the end of the Diablo era after more than a decade of production.

Murcie-1.short: This is actually a very cool Transformer by RoadBot, it can turn into a Robot or a Gorilla even!
Murcie-1.long: The Lamborghini Murciélago 6.2 was introduced in 2001 as the successor to the Diablo and became the first new Lamborghini developed under Audi ownership. It retained the dramatic V12 flagship formula of earlier Lamborghinis but combined it with significantly improved reliability, build quality, and usability. Powered by a 6.2-liter naturally aspirated V12 producing around 580 hp, the Murciélago featured all-wheel drive, active rear air intakes, and a design by Luc Donckerwolke that modernized Lamborghini styling while still referencing the Countach and Diablo. The car quickly became one of the defining supercars of the 2000s and the final Lamborghini V12 available with a traditional gated manual transmission.

MurcieAnni-1.long: The Lamborghini Murciélago 40th Anniversary was introduced in 2003 to celebrate 40 years of Automobili Lamborghini. Only 50 examples were built, all finished in the exclusive Verde Artemis pearl paint with carbon fiber exterior trim, anthracite wheels, silver brake calipers, and a uniquely styled asymmetric interior. The special edition also featured revised exhaust components, numbered plaques, and special anniversary details throughout the cabin. Today it is considered one of the rarest and most collectible early Murciélagos.

MurcieRGT-1.short: A pretty old Murcielago R-GT model I treated as a toy long time ago. I've always loved the livery, though.
MurcieRGT-1.long: The Lamborghini Murciélago R-GT entered competition in 2004 as Lamborghini's first serious factory-backed GT race car of the modern era. Developed with Reiter Engineering, it competed in FIA GT, the American Le Mans Series, and other endurance championships. The car used a racing version of the 6.0-liter V12 producing around 600 hp and featured extensive lightweight construction, race aerodynamics, and rear-wheel drive. Although reliability issues initially limited its success, the R-GT eventually achieved race victories and podiums, becoming the foundation for Lamborghini's future GT racing programs.

MurcieRGTPr-1._model: 2004 Lamborghini Murcielago R-GT Presentation | 1:64 | Black | Top Mark/LAMBOMODELS
MurcieRGTPr-1.short: This is a quick custom model I've made of the 2003 R-GT Presentation car, which had a few differences compared to the actual race cars. I quite like it for what it is!
MurcieRGTPr-1.long: The Lamborghini Murciélago R-GT Presentation Car was unveiled in 2003 as Lamborghini's official return to international GT racing. Developed together with Reiter Engineering and Audi Sport, the presentation car previewed Lamborghini's FIA GT and Le Mans ambitions. It featured a heavily widened body, massive aerodynamic modifications, racing suspension, stripped interior, and a rear-wheel-drive layout replacing the standard AWD system to comply with GT racing regulations. The presentation car directly evolved into the actual competition R-GT racers that entered FIA GT competition the following year.

MurcieRoad-1.short: A weird tuned Murcielago Roadster by Maisto, though, it's quite heavy and looks good from certain angles. Just not a fan of the wheels...
MurcieRoad-1.long: The Lamborghini Murciélago Roadster debuted in 2004 and transformed the coupe into a dramatic open-top supercar. Unlike a traditional convertible, it used a removable fabric roof panel intended only for occasional use at low speeds. The Roadster featured revised rear bodywork, asymmetrical engine-cover vents, and a redesigned rear deck optimized for cooling and structural rigidity. Powered by the same 6.2-liter V12 as the coupe, it became one of the most visually recognizable Murciélago variants ever produced.

MiuConcept-1._model: 2006 Lamborghini Miura Concept | 1:43 | Yellow | Mondo Motors
MiuConcept-1.short: This Miura concept model car by Mondo Motors actually features an interior, which the real car doesn't have!
MiuConcept-1.long: The 2006 Lamborghini Miura Concept was created to celebrate the 40th anniversary of the original Miura, reimagining one of Lamborghini's most iconic designs for the modern era. Designed by Walter de Silva, then head of Lamborghini and Audi Group design, it became the first Lamborghini developed under his direction and was internally known as "Concept M." Although heavily inspired by the original 1966 Miura designed by Marcello Gandini, the concept was actually based on the mechanical platform of the Murciélago, using a modern V12 layout and contemporary engineering underneath its retro-inspired bodywork. The design carefully preserved classic Miura elements such as the flowing proportions, side air intakes, louvered rear section, and distinctive "eyelash" headlight details, while subtly integrating modern Lamborghini styling cues. The car debuted in early 2006 and immediately generated enormous interest among enthusiasts, many hoping it would enter production. However, Lamborghini CEO Stephan Winkelmann made it clear that the Miura Concept would remain a one-off design study, stating that Lamborghini was focused on the future rather than retro production models. Despite never reaching production, the Miura Concept remains one of the most celebrated modern Lamborghini concepts and is now preserved in the Lamborghini Museum in Sant'Agata Bolognese.

MurcieBarchConc-1._model: 2006 Lamborghini Murcielago Barchetta Concept | 1:43 | Orange | AUTOart
MurcieBarchConc-1.short: An absolutely incredible model of the Murcielago Concept car by AUTOart. Though the original car was finished in Giallo paint, this one is finished in a very nice Orange color. Very detailed and realistic.
MurcieBarchConc-1.long:
The Lamborghini Murciélago Barchetta Concept was unveiled in 2002 as an early open-top design study previewing the future Murciélago Roadster. Unlike the later production Roadster, the Barchetta concept featured a far more aggressive and experimental design, including a chopped windshield, redesigned engine cover, special wheels, lightweight interior elements, and additional chassis reinforcement visible beneath the rear bodywork. Lamborghini also hinted that some of the styling details could influence the brand's future design language under Luc Donckerwolke.

Mechanically, the concept retained the standard Murciélago's 6.2-liter V12 producing around 580 hp with all-wheel drive and a 6-speed manual transmission. Although the Barchetta itself never entered production in this exact form, it directly previewed the production Murciélago Roadster that arrived shortly afterward.

Murcie640-1.short: My third ever Lamborghini model. I've always wanted a model of the Murcielago, ever since I got my first Lamborghini, the Diablo and the second one, which was the Aventador by Maisto. When I saw this model locally at a toy kiosk, I immediately wanted it. Safe to say I managed to convice my parents to get it for me. Great memories. I prefer the design of the original Murcielago over this LP640, though.
Murcie640-1.long: The Lamborghini Murciélago LP640 arrived in 2006 as a major facelift and mechanical upgrade of the original Murciélago. The "LP640" name referred to the longitudinal rear engine layout and its new output of 640 metric horsepower. The enlarged 6.5-liter V12 dramatically improved performance, while the car also received redesigned bumpers, revised headlights, larger air intakes, improved cooling, and a significantly upgraded interior. The LP640 is widely considered the definitive standard Murciélago and one of the greatest manual V12 Lamborghinis ever built.

Murcie640Road-1.long: The Lamborghini Murciélago LP640 Roadster combined the updated LP640 platform with the dramatic open-top design of the earlier Roadster. Introduced in 2006, it featured the upgraded 640 hp 6.5-liter V12 along with revised styling, improved interior quality, and enhanced performance. The removable soft roof remained extremely minimalistic, reinforcing the Roadster's raw and theatrical character. The LP640 Roadster became one of the most iconic convertible Lamborghinis of the 2000s.

MurcieSV-1.long: The Lamborghini Murciélago LP670-4 SuperVeloce, introduced in 2009, became the ultimate and final evolution of the Murciélago. The "SV" name returned for the first time since the Diablo era and focused on maximum performance through weight reduction and increased power. The 6.5-liter V12 was upgraded to 670 hp, while carbon fiber components, a stripped interior, revised aerodynamics, and a huge optional rear wing reduced weight by around 100 kg compared to the LP640. The LP670-4 SV became one of Lamborghini's most legendary modern V12 cars and marked the end of the Murciélago generation before the Aventador replaced it.

MurcieSVCN-1.long: The Lamborghini Murciélago LP670-4 SV China Limited Edition was a special series of only 10 cars created exclusively for the Chinese market in 2010. Based on the standard LP670-4 SV, the cars featured a unique two-tone black and orange livery inspired by traditional Chinese colors, along with special badging and numbered plaques. The limited edition celebrated Lamborghini's growing presence in China and remains one of the rarest Murciélago variants ever built.
